nativeRange.habitat: Adults tend to inhabit lagoon and seaward reefs, often staying close to the bottom among rubble beds, at depths of approximately 1 to 20 metres.

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on zooplankton and, to a lesser extent, benthic algae in the wild. In captivity, it has been known to accept a wide variety of meaty foods, herbivore preparations, and flaked foods.

imageCandidates.notes: Rickard Zerpe; 2185x1639. Tack-sharp full-body side profile with vivid electric-blue upper body and yellow lower body/tail against clean rubble bokeh; textbook diagnostic coloration.
Rickard Zerpe; 1959x1469. Sharp whole-fish shot facing right showing brilliant blue body with strongly saturated orange-yellow tail and anal fin; good angle variety.
Rickard Zerpe; 1720x1290. Sharp in-situ shot among live coral showing blue body, yellow belly and white-tipped fins; natural reef context.

summary: A vibrant electric-blue-and-yellow damselfish from the Indo-Pacific, generally considered beginner-friendly but has been known to be confrontational toward tankmates; best kept singly in a reef with ample hiding spots.
careNotes: The Blue and Gold Damselfish displays electric blue on the upper body with bright yellow on the lower body and posterior — coloration can vary considerably between individuals. They tend to spend much of their time darting in and out of holes and crevices, particularly in well-lit tanks. Despite being reef-safe, they have been reported to be aggressive toward other fish, especially conspecifics and other damselfish; keeping them singly is generally advised. In the wild, juveniles have been documented schooling among soft corals while adults form small groups or larger congregations near rubble beds.
breedingNotes: In the wild, this species is oviparous with distinct pairing behavior; males have been documented guarding and aerating eggs. Captive breeding has not been widely reported in the aquarium hobby literature consulted.

fish.breedingNotes: This species reproduces by laying eggs (oviparous), and distinct pairing behavior has been observed in the wild, with males taking responsibility for guarding and aerating the eggs. Captive breeding has rarely been documented in the hobby.
